startScreenOptions.title: BI4.1-BI4.8 — Interactive Lecture (BI)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 5 embedded questions for BI4.1-BI4.8.

interactions.label: Phospholipids, glycolipids, and lipoproteins are grouped together as compound li

params.question: Phospholipids, glycolipids, and lipoproteins are grouped together as compound lipids because they all:

answers.text: Are esters of fatty acids with alcohols only

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This describes simple lipids such as triglycerides. Compound lipids carry an additional chemical group beyond the fatty acid–alcohol ester.

answers.text: Contain a fatty acid component along with an additional chemical group

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Compound lipids are distinguished by an extra moiety — a phosphate group (phospholipids), a sugar (glycolipids), or a protein shell (lipoproteins) — that confers specific biological functions.

answers.text: Are produced by the breakdown of simple lipids

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Products of lipid breakdown (e.g., fatty acids, cholesterol, prostaglandins) are classified as derived lipids, not compound lipids.

answers.text: Dissolve freely in water because of their polar head groups

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Although compound lipids have amphipathic regions, they remain insoluble in water — the defining physical property of all lipids. Their classification rests on having an additional chemical group, not on solubility.

overallFeedback.feedback: Recall the three main categories: simple lipids (esters of FA + alcohol), compound lipids (FA + alcohol + an additional group such as phosphate or sugar), and derived lipids (e.g., cholesterol, fatty acids, prostaglandins).
Compound lipids carry fatty acids plus an extra group — phosphate, sugar, or protein — which distinguishes them from simple lipids (FA + alcohol only) and derived lipids (breakdown products).

interactions.label: Malonyl-CoA inhibits carnitine palmitoyltransferase I (CPT-I). What is the metab

params.question: Malonyl-CoA inhibits carnitine palmitoyltransferase I (CPT-I). What is the metabolic purpose of this inhibition?

answers.text: It allows fatty acids to enter the mitochondrial matrix without carnitine

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Long-chain acyl-CoA always requires the carnitine shuttle for mitochondrial entry. Malonyl-CoA blocks this entry rather than bypassing it.

answers.text: It prevents fatty acid synthesis and beta-oxidation from running simultaneously

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Malonyl-CoA is an intermediate of fatty acid synthesis. By inhibiting CPT-I, it blocks fatty acid entry into mitochondria for oxidation, preventing the futile cycle of building and breaking down fatty acids at the same time.

answers.text: It directs acetyl-CoA towards the Krebs cycle instead of ketogenesis

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The fate of acetyl-CoA within the mitochondrial matrix is regulated by different mechanisms. Malonyl-CoA controls fatty acid entry into mitochondria, not the routing of acetyl-CoA once inside.

answers.text: It ensures medium-chain fatty acids are oxidised preferentially over long-chain ones

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Medium-chain fatty acids can bypass the carnitine shuttle entirely and enter mitochondria independently. Malonyl-CoA's regulatory role targets long-chain fatty acid entry via CPT-I.

overallFeedback.feedback: CPT-I is the rate-limiting gatekeeper for fatty acid entry into mitochondria. Malonyl-CoA, generated during fatty acid synthesis, inhibits CPT-I so that the cell does not synthesise and oxidise fatty acids at the same time.
Malonyl-CoA acts as a metabolic switch — produced during lipogenesis, it inhibits CPT-I to prevent the energetically wasteful cycle of simultaneous fat synthesis and fat breakdown.

interactions.label: When intracellular cholesterol levels fall, the sterol regulatory element-bindin

params.question: When intracellular cholesterol levels fall, the sterol regulatory element-binding protein (SREBP) translocates to the nucleus. Which of the following best describes the consequence?

answers.text: Transcription of HMG-CoA reductase and LDL receptor genes is activated

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Low cholesterol releases SREBP from the ER membrane. In the nucleus it activates genes for HMG-CoA reductase (boosting cholesterol synthesis) and LDL receptors (increasing uptake of LDL cholesterol from blood). Statins exploit this same mechanism.

answers.text: HMG-CoA reductase activity is immediately inhibited to conserve substrates

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The opposite occurs. When cholesterol is low, SREBP increases HMG-CoA reductase expression to boost synthesis, not inhibit it.

answers.text: Bile acid synthesis in hepatocytes is suppressed to retain cholesterol

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Bile acid synthesis is the main route of cholesterol disposal, but it is not directly regulated by SREBP nuclear translocation. SREBP primarily controls synthesis and receptor-mediated uptake.

answers.text: Cholesterol is esterified and stored in cytoplasmic lipid droplets

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Cholesterol esterification by ACAT stores excess cholesterol. When levels are low, the priority is to increase synthesis and uptake — the opposite of storage.

overallFeedback.feedback: When cholesterol is low, SREBP moves to the nucleus and switches on genes for HMG-CoA reductase and LDL receptors. When cholesterol is high, SREBP stays anchored at the ER and transcription stops.

SREBP is the master cholesterol sensor. Low levels free it to the nucleus, where it simultaneously upregulates HMG-CoA reductase (synthesis) and LDL receptors (uptake) — the pathway that statins exploit therapeutically.

interactions.label: Low-density lipoprotein (LDL) performs reverse cholesterol transport by collecti

params.question: Low-density lipoprotein (LDL) performs reverse cholesterol transport by collecting excess cholesterol from peripheral tissues and returning it to the liver.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: LDL delivers cholesterol to peripheral tissues — it is called 'bad cholesterol' because excess deposits accumulate in artery walls. Reverse cholesterol transport — collecting surplus cholesterol and returning it to the liver — is the protective function of HDL, the 'good cholesterol'.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — the statement is false. Reverse cholesterol transport is performed by high-density lipoprotein (HDL), not LDL. LDL does the opposite: it delivers cholesterol to peripheral tissues. When LDL is elevated or its receptor is deficient, cholesterol accumulates in artery walls, driving atherosclerosis.

interactions.label: In the development of atherosclerosis, which cells engulf oxidised LDL through s

params.question: In the development of atherosclerosis, which cells engulf oxidised LDL through scavenger receptors to become foam cells?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Endothelial cells line the artery wall and are the first to be injured, but they do not take up oxidised LDL to become foam cells.
Smooth muscle cells migrate into the intima and secrete the fibrous cap over the lipid core, but the characteristic foam cells of early atherosclerosis arise from macrophages.

Correct. Macrophages recognise oxidised LDL via scavenger receptors, which — unlike standard LDL receptors — are never downregulated by rising intracellular cholesterol. This unchecked uptake transforms macrophages into lipid-laden foam cells, the hallmark of the fatty streak.

Platelets aggregate on a ruptured plaque to form the thrombus that can block the artery. Foam cell formation, however, is driven by macrophages engulfing oxidised LDL in the earlier stages of plaque development.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the atherosclerosis sequence: endothelial injury → LDL entry and oxidation → macrophage uptake via scavenger receptors → foam cells → fatty streak → fibrous plaque → rupture and thrombosis.

Macrophage scavenger receptors are never switched off by rising cholesterol — unlike normal LDL receptors — allowing unlimited uptake of oxidised LDL and transformation into foam cells, the earliest visible lesion of atherosclerosis.
